meta.title: 1.3 Components Inside Components
meta.description: Pages are built by nesting components like building blocks.

blocks.prompt: Think about a webpage you use every day -- maybe Gmail, Twitter, or a company dashboard. If you had to break it into separate pieces (a header, a sidebar, a list of items, a footer), how many pieces would there be? Write down a rough list of the pieces you can identify.

blocks.question:
In this JSX, how many React components and how many HTML elements are there? ```jsx
```
blocks.answer: There are 3 React components (Header, SearchBar, ResultsList -- all capitalized) and 2 HTML elements (div, main -- all lowercase). The capital letter rule makes this distinction immediate: capitalized tags are custom components defined somewhere in the codebase, lowercase tags are standard HTML elements.

problems.question:
Which of the following are React components (not HTML elements) in this JSX? ```jsx return (
); ```
options: C) PageTitle, ProductCard, Copyright, section, div, footer
problems.explanation: The capital letter rule: PageTitle, ProductCard, and Copyright start with capital letters, so they are React components. section, div, and footer start with lowercase letters, so they are standard HTML elements. Note that ProductCard appears twice -- it is the same component used in two places.

options: A) In a file at ./components/Sidebar.js or .jsx or .tsx
B) In a file at ../shared/Sidebar.js or .jsx or .tsx
C) In the same file, below the import statements
problems.explanation: The import path tells you exactly where the file is. 'import Sidebar from "../shared/Sidebar"' means the Sidebar component is in a file called Sidebar (with a .js, .jsx, or .tsx extension) inside a 'shared' folder one directory up from the current file. The '..' means 'go up one directory.'

options: A) The page has a progress bar showing step 3, then an order summary next to a payment form, and a help link at the bottom
B) The page only shows a payment form -- the other components are hidden
C) You cannot tell anything about the page without reading each component's code
problems.explanation: Component names are descriptive. ProgressBar with step={3} likely shows the user is on step 3. OrderSummary and PaymentForm sit side by side in a checkout-body container. HelpLink is at the bottom. You do not need to read each component's code to understand the page layout -- the names and structure tell you the story. This is the power of well-named components.

blocks.prompt: Explain component composition in your own words. How do you tell the difference between a React component and an HTML element in JSX? If you see a component you do not recognize, how do you find where it is defined?

blocks.content: React pages are built by nesting components inside other components. Capital letters mean React components, lowercase means HTML elements. Import statements tell you where each component is defined. You can understand a page's structure from component names and arrangement without reading every component's code.

blocks.prompt: You can now read JSX, identify the structure of a component, and understand how components nest together to form pages. The Level 1 checkpoint will test all three skills together -- reading complete (small) components and identifying what they render.

reviewQuestions.question: How do you tell the difference between a React component and an HTML element in JSX?
options: React components start with a capital letter, HTML elements are lowercase
React components always have a className attribute
reviewQuestions.explanation: The capital letter rule: tags starting with a capital letter (like <Header />) are React components, while lowercase tags (like <div>) are standard HTML elements.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: You've got it! The capital letter rule is one of the most useful patterns for reading React code quickly.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Remember the capital letter rule: <Header /> is a React component (capital H), while <div> is plain HTML (lowercase d).

reviewQuestions.question: If you see '<Sidebar />' used inside a component, where would you look to find its code?
options: In the import statements at the top of the file
In the return statement of the same component
reviewQuestions.explanation: Import statements at the top of the file tell you the file path where each component is defined. You follow the import to find the source code.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Exactly right! The imports are your roadmap for navigating a React codebase.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Good thinking, but the answer is the import statements. Something like 'import Sidebar from ./components/Sidebar' tells you exactly where to find the code.
